questions.stem: In the lecture's list of AI agent characteristics, "goal-directedness" means that the agent:
options: Works toward an objective rather than just responding to a single prompt
Requires explicit human approval before every step it takes toward a goal
Can only pursue objectives that appeared in its training data
Restates the user's goal at the start of every LLM invocation
questions.explanation: Goal-directedness means the agent pursues an objective across multiple steps instead of one-shot prompt answering. Operating with limited human input belongs to a different characteristic — autonomy.

questions.stem: The lecture notes there are differences of opinion about what exactly constitutes an AI agent. Which characteristic is sometimes EXCLUDED from the definition?
options: Autonomy — reasoning and deciding with limited human input
Adaptability — responding to scenarios it has not seen before
Improving — learning and getting better over time
Goal-directedness — working toward an objective
questions.explanation: The slide explicitly notes that "Improving" is sometimes not included in definitions of an AI agent, while goal-directedness, autonomy, adaptability, and collaboration are core.

questions.stem: In the agent architecture presented in class, which component interprets inputs, decides what to do next, and generates output — and is usually implemented using an LLM?
options: The planner
The top-level supervisor ("brain")
The memory store
The tool dispatcher
questions.explanation: The top-level supervisor or "brain" interprets inputs, decides the next step, and generates output, usually via an LLM. The planner's narrower job is breaking complex goals into smaller tasks.

questions.stem: According to the lecture, an agent's memory component helps store:
options: The fine-tuned model weights the agent has learned for its domain
The agent's internal logs, including past plans, actions, and agent–user interactions
Only the JSON tool schemas registered with the LLM
A read-only snapshot of the external environment taken at startup
questions.explanation: Memory stores the agent's internal logs — past plans, actions, and interactions between agent and user. It is not about model weights or static tool schemas.

questions.stem: In the lecture's travel-assistant comparison, what distinguishes the agent-based solution from using a plain LLM?
options: The agent uses a larger context window to compare many more flight options
The agent is fine-tuned on airline data, so it finds cheaper flights
The agent asks the user many more clarifying questions before answering
The agent itself checks the calendar and books the flights, using the user's stored preferences
questions.explanation: With a plain LLM the user must check the calendar and do the booking; the agent executes the whole task in place of the human, using known preferences and frequent-flier details. The difference is action, not model size or tuning.

questions.stem: A key difference the lecture draws between LangGraph agents and modern function-calling AI agents concerns dynamicity. What is it?
options: LangGraph paths are predefined at design time; a function-calling agent's supervisor determines the next task dynamically at run time
LangGraph cannot make any run-time decisions, whereas AI agents fix all their decisions at design time
LangGraph supports tools, while function-calling agents can only generate plain text
Function-calling agents require a fixed order of tool invocations, while LangGraph chooses tools freely
questions.explanation: LangGraph can choose which edge to take at run time, but always within a predefined set of program paths. A function-calling agent examines the outcome of actions and decides the next step dynamically, so the possible execution sequences are not fixed in advance.

questions.stem: One capability that differentiates most AI agents from a plain LLM is:
options: The ability to reason about a problem step by step
A substantially larger context window
The ability to invoke tools that act on external systems
A built-in debugger for inspecting the model's reasoning
questions.explanation: Plain LLMs can already reason step by step, so that does not distinguish agents. Tool invocation is what lets an agent act on its environment rather than only produce text.

questions.stem: In the ReAct technique, the agent's repeating cycle is:
options: action → observation → back to action
reasoning → action → observation → back to reasoning
reasoning → action → observation → verification → back to reasoning
observation → verification → action → back to observation
questions.explanation: ReAct first thinks (plans) what to do, executes the action, observes how the environment changed, and repeats. There is no separate verification phase in the cycle — that is the tempting over-elaboration.

questions.stem: In ReAct, what is the key difference between a thought and an action?
options: Thoughts are produced by the planner while actions are produced by memory
Actions are only used for physical robots; thoughts handle software tasks
Thoughts must be validated by a human before any action executes
Actions affect the external environment; thoughts only update the model's internal state and context
questions.explanation: Actions change the external environment and lead to observations; reasoning traces (thoughts) do not touch the environment — they update the internal context to support future reasoning and acting.

questions.stem: A ReAct-style agent tells a robot to move to the staircase, then checks whether the robot actually arrived there before deciding what to do next. Checking the robot's location corresponds to which ReAct step?
options: Observation
Action
Thought (reasoning)
Self-reflection
questions.explanation: Seeing whether the robot reached the right location is observing the effect of the previous action on the environment. The instruction to move was the action, and deciding what to do next is the thought.

questions.stem: In ReAct terminology, which of the following is an ACTION rather than a thought?
options: Reasoning that a fact needs to be looked up on the Internet
Concluding that the last search result answers the question
Invoking an Internet search tool
Deciding which sub-goal to pursue next
questions.explanation: Actually invoking the search tool acts on the external environment, so it is an action. Deciding a fact is needed, judging results, and choosing sub-goals are all reasoning steps that only change internal context.

questions.stem: When an agent uses reflection, it typically asks the LLM to reflect on:
options: Whether additional tools should be added to its tool set
The current state and what went wrong in the last action
A solution proposed by a different agent, in order to critique it
What would have happened had it taken a different action
questions.explanation: Reflection has the LLM examine the current state and consider what went wrong in the last action; that verbal description is fed into the next prompt to drive a better plan. Critiquing another agent's solution is a different technique (agent critique).

questions.stem: In the Reflexion framework, the Evaluator's job is to:
options: Choose the next action to take by consulting an LLM
Generate verbal self-reflections to feed into future trials
Break the overall task down into smaller sub-tasks
Observe the environment after an action and assign it a reward score
questions.explanation: The Evaluator observes the outcome of an action and decides on a reward score, which may be binary (success/fail) or more complex. Choosing actions is the Actor's job; generating verbal feedback is the Self-reflection component.

questions.stem: How does Reflexion enable an agent to improve across trials WITHOUT updating any model weights?
options: It stores verbal self-reflections about what went wrong in memory and uses them in future trials
It applies small gradient updates to the LLM after each failed trial
It maintains a tree of candidate solutions and expands the highest-scoring branch
It trains a separate reward model that filters the Actor's proposed actions
questions.explanation: Reflexion's "verbal reinforcement learning" generates natural-language self-reflections that are stored in the agent's memory and consulted in future trials — no gradient updates or separate trained models are involved. Tree expansion describes MCTS, not Reflexion.

questions.stem: Which method is the lecture's example of the "multiple candidates" adaptation technique, where many candidates are maintained and ranked at each step to pick the most promising one to refine?
options: Multi-Agent Debate (MAD)
Reflexion
Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)
Self-organizing agents
questions.explanation: MCTS is the example of maintaining and ranking multiple candidates. MAD exemplifies agent critique, and self-organizing agents exemplify divide-and-conquer.

questions.stem: The lecture describes modern LLM function calling as a concrete instantiation of which conceptual framework?
options: Reflexion
Chain-of-Thought prompting
Monte-Carlo Tree Search
ReAct
questions.explanation: Function calling (introduced by GPT in 2023, also called tool calling) directly instantiates ReAct's interleaving of thoughts, actions, and observations inside the LLM API.

questions.stem: Why can't an LLM directly execute a function call, such as invoking a currency-exchange API?
options: API providers block network traffic that originates from LLM vendors
An LLM only predicts text — it can only tell the caller which function to invoke and with what parameters
Executing a function would overflow the LLM's context window
LLMs can execute functions directly, but only functions written in Python
questions.explanation: LLMs are language models predicting text; they cannot run code. Function-calling support means the LLM returns the exact function name and parameter values, and the caller (the agent/supervisor code) performs the invocation.

questions.stem: In a tool schema passed to the LLM, the lecture calls the description field crucial because it:
options: Tells the LLM when it should invoke that function
Is displayed to the end user before each tool runs
Determines the JSON types of the function's parameters
Is used by the runtime to locate the function's source code
questions.explanation: The description is how the LLM decides when a tool is relevant to the request, which drives function selection. Parameter types are set by the separate parameters schema, and the runtime dispatches by name, not description.

questions.stem: In a GPT tool definition, what is the purpose of setting "strict": true together with "additionalProperties": false?
options: It forces the model to emit a tool call on every single turn
It makes all parameters optional so the model can omit any of them
It prevents the model from adding extra, non-existent parameters to the function call
It caps how many tool calls the model may return in one response
questions.explanation: These settings constrain the model to the declared parameter schema, preventing it from inventing extra parameters that the real function does not accept. They do not control how often tools are called.

questions.stem: An LLM's function-calling response contains a list of several tool calls [f1, f2, …, fn], with no dependencies among them. According to the lecture, the LLM is advising the caller to:
options: Invoke only f1 and discard the remaining suggestions
Ask the LLM in a follow-up prompt which single call to keep
Invoke them strictly one at a time, with an LLM round-trip between each
Invoke all of the functions in parallel
questions.explanation: When multiple functions are returned, the LLM is advising the caller to invoke all of them in parallel; only when fj depends on fi must fi be invoked first.

questions.stem: You ask "What's the weather in Paris?" and your get_weather tool takes latitude and longitude. The LLM's tool call arrives with arguments {"latitude": 48.8566, "longitude": 2.3522}. This illustrates that the LLM:
options: Executed the tool itself and cached the coordinates for reuse
Performed parameter mapping — converting "Paris" into coordinate values itself
Silently called a separate geolocation tool before answering
Requires the user to supply coordinates explicitly in the prompt
questions.explanation: The LLM computed appropriate parameter values from its own knowledge, converting the city name into coordinates — the parameter-mapping step of function calling. No tool was executed by the LLM itself.

questions.stem: After your agent executes the function the LLM requested, what does the lecture say you should do with the result?
options: Append it as a role "tool" message and call the LLM again — giving the observation back
Print it to the user, since the LLM's part of the interaction is finished
Re-send the original prompt with the result substituted into the system message
Store it in a vector database so future queries can retrieve it
questions.explanation: The result is appended to the message list as a role:"tool" message (with the matching tool_call_id) and the LLM is invoked again — in ReAct terminology, you are handing the observation back so it can pick the next action or produce the final answer.

questions.stem: In the agentic (tool-calling) loop presented in class — the pattern used by Claude Code and Cursor — the loop terminates when:
options: The dispatcher runs out of registered tools to try
Every available tool has been invoked at least once
The problem is solved or an iteration limit is reached
The user manually confirms each tool result
questions.explanation: The agentic loop (sometimes called the ReAct cycle) keeps prompting the LLM and dispatching tool calls until the problem is solved or a limit is reached. It has nothing to do with exhausting the tool list.

questions.stem: The lecture breaks function calling into five steps. Which step computes the appropriate argument values to pass to the chosen function?
options: Intent recognition
Function selection
Response generation
Parameter mapping
questions.explanation: Parameter mapping computes the argument values. Intent recognition detects that a tool is needed, function selection picks the best tool, and response generation folds the function output into the reply to the user.

questions.stem: An agent enforces separate LLM-call and tool-call budgets. Its main loop calls the LLM 6 times; along the way it dispatches an LLM-backed image-extraction tool once, an LLM-backed text-to-SQL tool twice, and a pure-Python calculator once. What do the two counters read at the end?
options: 9 LLM calls and 4 tool calls
6 LLM calls and 4 tool calls
6 LLM calls and 9 tool calls
9 LLM calls and 9 tool calls
questions.explanation: Each LLM-backed tool counts as 1 tool call AND 1 internal LLM call, so the LLM counter is 6 + 1 + 2 = 9 while the tool counter is 1 + 2 + 1 = 4. Forgetting the tool-internal LLM calls gives the tempting 6/4 answer.

questions.stem: In a tool-calling agentic loop, what signals that the LLM considers the task solved and the loop should exit successfully?
options: The most recent tool returns a success confirmation string
The LLM returns an assistant message containing no tool calls
The tool-call counter reaches its configured maximum
A separate validation function checks the output and returns True
questions.explanation: In the agentic loop, the LLM either names the next tool to invoke or returns a final answer; an assistant message with no tool calls is that final answer, ending the loop. Hitting a call limit is a failure exit, and tool confirmations or external validators do not drive the loop's control flow.

questions.stem: Mid-run, an agent's SQL execution tool returns [{"error": "no such column: total"}]. What is the BEST way for the agentic loop to handle this?
options: Terminate the loop immediately and report the run as failed
Silently re-execute the identical SQL statement up to three times
Append the error as a role "tool" message so the LLM can revise the SQL and retry
Pause the loop and ask the user to correct the SQL before continuing
questions.explanation: Feeding the error back as a role "tool" message turns the failure into an observation the LLM can reason over, letting it regenerate corrected SQL on the next turn. Terminating or blindly re-running the same statement throws away that self-correction ability, and escalating to the user defeats the agent's autonomy.

questions.stem: An agent tracks its LLM calls and tool calls with separate counters. Dispatching which of these tools should increment BOTH counters?
options: A calculator tool that safely evaluates arithmetic expressions in pure Python
A tool that executes a given SQL string against a read-only SQLite database
A tool that writes the given text content to a file on disk
A tool that translates a natural-language request into SQL by prompting an LLM
questions.explanation: A text-to-SQL tool wraps its own LLM call, so dispatching it consumes one tool call plus one internal LLM call. Calculators, SQL executors, and file writers are deterministic code with no model inside, so they count only against the tool budget.

questions.stem: An agent is given a deterministic calculator tool even though the LLM could answer arithmetic questions directly in its text response. The main rationale for this design is that:
options: LLMs fabricate plausible but wrong arithmetic, and the tool removes that failure mode
Numeric answers produced as plain text cannot be parsed back into the conversation
Tool-calling APIs require the model to invoke at least one tool on every turn
Arithmetic done via a tool call does not consume any context-window tokens
questions.explanation: An LLM predicts tokens rather than computing, so it can produce confident but incorrect arithmetic; delegating math to a deterministic tool eliminates that failure mode. The other options are false: text numbers parse fine, tool calls are always optional per turn, and tool calls and their results still consume context tokens.

questions.stem: A tool-calling agent keeps requesting the same tool over and over, burning through its turn budget without progress. The PRIMARY fix is to:
options: Raise the tool-call budget so the agent has more turns to escape the loop
Wrap the loop in a framework-managed agent class that handles control flow automatically
Tighten the tool schema descriptions, since they teach the model when NOT to call a tool
Lower the sampling temperature to zero so the model stops exploring alternatives
questions.explanation: Schema descriptions are the primary way the model learns when a tool is — and is not — appropriate, so sharpening them attacks the root cause of repeated calls. Raising the budget or swapping in a framework gives the same confusion more room to loop, and temperature zero makes a deterministic repeat more likely, not less.

questions.stem: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard for connecting data sources and AI-powered tools. It was open-sourced in November 2024 by:
options: OpenAI
Anthropic
Google
Meta
questions.explanation: MCP was open-sourced by Anthropic in November 2024 as an open standard for secure, two-way connections between data sources and AI applications.

questions.stem: According to the lecture, MCP servers can provide which three main types of capabilities?
options: Models, embeddings, and datasets
Agents, planners, and memories
Chains, graphs, and nodes
Resources, tools, and prompts
questions.explanation: MCP servers expose resources (data the LLM can read), tools (functions the LLM can call on the server), and prompts (reusable templates), each discoverable via its own list endpoint.

questions.stem: The lecture presents MCP as an extension of function calling. What does MCP mainly add?
options: Dynamic discovery of new tools and the ability to invoke remote tools
Guaranteed deterministic ordering of tool invocations
Automatic fine-tuning of the LLM on the outputs of each tool
A sandbox that executes tools inside the LLM itself
questions.explanation: With plain function calling the tool set is fixed and local; MCP generalizes it with dynamic tool discovery (tools/list) and remote invocation, plus resources and prompts.

questions.stem: Your MCP-based shopping agent quotes prices in dollars. You add a currency-conversion tool to the MCP server, changing nothing in the agent application itself. Why can users now ask for prices in euros?
options: The server rewrites the agent's system prompt to mention currency support
MCP servers translate currency values natively at the transport layer
The agent discovers the new tool dynamically from the server's tool list, and the LLM invokes it when relevant
The MCP client caches exchange rates gathered in previous sessions
questions.explanation: This is the lecture's "power of dynamicity" example: because clients discover server tools at run time, adding a tool to the server augments the application without changing the app — the LLM will pick it up when the user's request needs it.

questions.stem: In the lecture's MCP shopping-agent example, the server's tools access a product microservice backend. What does the architecture say about that backend?
options: The MCP client connects to it directly once the tools are discovered
It is not exposed to the client — the MCP server wraps it as an intermediary
It must itself be an MCP server for the tools to reach it
The LLM queries it directly through the resources/list endpoint
questions.explanation: The server's tools access the product microservice, but the backend is never exposed to the client — MCP servers act as wrappers or intermediaries providing a standardized way to reach external systems.

questions.stem: As the number of MCP servers and tools grows very large, giving the LLM all the tools degrades performance. How does the MCP-Zero framework address this?
options: It fine-tunes a small router model on historical tool-usage logs
It limits every MCP server to exposing at most ten tools
It requires human approval before each new tool is registered
The agent requests the type of tool it needs, then hierarchical matching runs against server and tool descriptions
questions.explanation: MCP-Zero lets the agent actively request what type of tool it wants and then performs hierarchical matching — first against the server description, then the tool description — instead of stuffing every tool into the context.

questions.stem: Which of the following does the lecture list as a common way agents using function calling misbehave?
options: Invoking tools faster than API rate limits allow
Refusing to act without human confirmation at every step
Getting stuck repeating the same actions over and over
Always preferring the cheapest tool regardless of the task
questions.explanation: Common failure modes include getting stuck repeating actions, ignoring updated observations such as error messages or test failures, and choosing an incorrect next step even when the right one is obvious.

questions.stem: Your SWE agent reports that the test suite passed, but its trace shows it never actually ran the tests — it invented the tool output. In the taxonomy from "When Agents Go Astray", this is classified as:
options: A hallucination — a reasoning error
Step repetition — a specification error
Task derailment — a coordination error
A role specification violation — a specification error
questions.explanation: Fabricating results or tool outputs is a hallucination, which the taxonomy groups under reasoning errors (decision-making failures). Verification failure would be neglecting to check work, not inventing results.

questions.stem: An agent has satisfied all the task's completion criteria but keeps issuing new tool calls instead of finalizing. In the "When Agents Go Astray" taxonomy, this behavior is:
options: Goal deviation — a coordination error
Termination condition unawareness — a specification error
Verification failure — a coordination error
Problem misidentification — a reasoning error
questions.explanation: Continuing to work after completion criteria are met is termination condition unawareness, a specification error; the suggested recovery is instructing the agent to finalize. Goal deviation means pursuing sub-goals that don't serve the main objective.

questions.stem: Gödel Agent is presented as a self-optimizing agent. According to the course, it improves its ability to solve problems by:
options: Rewording its prompts to elicit better LLM responses
Trying several predetermined strategies per problem and keeping the best one
Recording failed attempts in memory so it never repeats the same mistake
Continuously trying new approaches and accepting changes to its own code that improve performance
questions.explanation: Gödel Agent recursively improves itself by dynamically modifying its own logic and code (via introspection and monkey patching), keeping changes that raise performance. Prompt rewording and failure memories describe milder meta-learning approaches.

questions.stem: What distinguishes the Gödel Agent from a meta-learning optimized agent?
options: The Gödel Agent needs no utility function, while meta-learning depends on one
The Gödel Agent optimizes only during training, while meta-learning optimizes at run time
It recursively updates both its policy π AND its learning algorithm I; meta-learning keeps the learning algorithm fixed
The Gödel Agent avoids modifying code and relies purely on prompt optimization
questions.explanation: In meta-learning the learning algorithm I is fixed and only the policy π evolves during training. Gödel Agent recursively updates both π and I, rewriting its own code base after each update — both use a utility function U to score performance.

questions.stem: In the Gödel Agent algorithm, which action prompts the LLM to alter the agent's policy π and learning algorithm I, producing new versions (π′, I′)?
options: self update
self inspect
interact
continue improve
questions.explanation: self update asks the LLM to rewrite (π, I) into (π′, I′). self inspect only reads the current algorithm via introspection, interact calls the utility function U to score performance, and continue improve recursively invokes the decision algorithm.
